turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in Number oh oh seven ninety-nine, the City of Los Angeles versus Alameda Books, Inc. Mr. Klekner. [Inaudible]

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it may it please the Court. Following its nineteen seventy-seven pioneering study, the City of Los Angeles enacted a, in nineteen seventy-eight a comprehensive zoning ordinance regulating the location of all types of adult business activities in the city. The ordinance defined eight business activities and required that they each be separated from each other by one thousand feet. Notwithstanding the clear intent of the city's ordinance in nineteen seventy-eight, it became apparent that the ordinance could and was being interpreted to permit more than one adult business in the same building. This has been described by plaintiffs as a drafting error or an inad- inadvertent loophole, but it was an ambiguity, and it became necessary to amend the original ordinance in nineteen eighty-three to remove that ambiguity,

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: thereby... D- do you know...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: if, at the time the ordinance was enacted, nineteen seventy-seven?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: seventy-eight, sir.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: seventy-eight.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I presume...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: businesses existed in that form in nineteen seventy-eight?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: I believe they did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: study specifically analyzed specific combinations in one building.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: the purpose of the study.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: your clients didn't didn't have businesses like that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I'm sorry. They- the...

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The businesses regulated here did not offer both.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: I'm sorry, are you, b- b-, I did not hear the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: question entirely, sir.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: was initially passed, which of the two types of business did they offer?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Well, these businesses started in the late eighties, early nineteen nineties, either as a bookstore or as an adult arcade.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I'm asking, which which?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: well after the adoption of the ordinance.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Which did they start as, a bookstore, or as an...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: arcade?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: is that each w- I think it was Highland Books started as an adult arcade, and the reverse was true for Alameda Books.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Did your evidence in seventy-eight or any evidence since then specifically address the question of whether the combination of more than one form of business at one location has any measurable effect on, you know, the deleterious effect that you you measured?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Am I wrong in thinking that e- the study which you rely on, the nineteen seventy-seven study, did have as one category bookstore arcade.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The the answer is yes and no.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Well then, is there nothing to what we're told, that the this combination of video sales plus viewing is common and, indeed, an arcade can't survive as a stand-alone business, that the combination of, you can see it, and if you want it you can buy it, was common?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Well, the issue of separateness, and whether a business, an arcade and a bookstore can, could, or should survive separately is off the table.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Court's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: each of these each of these businesses was one or the other of those things and operated on that basis alone for how long?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Several years.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: , but I can't...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: they operated at a profit or at a loss?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: That We never did any discovery on that, so the answer is, we have no way of knowing.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But they did it for a couple of years, anyway?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: They were only an arcade and there was no selling going on when they were initially...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: launched?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: my understanding that they might have have sell e- s- excuse me, sold some books, or or or what-have-you, but their primary business was having a a series of video booths where you could watch adult movies.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I get the opposite impression.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Not based upon the facts as I understand them, sir.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Not based upon the facts as I understand them, that and I believe th- in in nineteen ninety-one it was Alameda Books that f- became both, and it was I think nineteen ninety-two that Highland Books became both.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: I'd I'd like to ask you I'll assume with you for the moment you don't need any evidence.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The theory is the solution, not the facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: dispersal of business...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: if in fact, there is no space limitation, I would repeat my question, that it seems a fortiori if you can have a bookstore of gigantic size, or an arcade of gigantic size, but you can't have the two together, even of a little size, so I don't I'm not talking about evidence.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The theory is simply the solution adopted by the city of Los Angeles was to disperse.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: five hundred...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: doesn't answer the question, exactly.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: But now we're talking about an ordinance that doesn't focus on the concentration of adult businesses but, rather, the combination, because the city doesn't want the business to break into two parts, even though it tried to operate as one business, and I guess the the nineteen seventy-seven study didn't address the combination, the effects of a combination of focuses in a single business, whereas your ordinance does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: concentration of businesses.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: There are several responses to the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: inaudible...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: operated as a single store, a single business.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: From the city's perspective, it is illogical, or not reasonable to say, for the city council to say, you know we have a problem here, on LaSeneca Boulevard, because we have too many adult businesses close together, so we're going to require them to disperse.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, what if the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: you can sell adult VHS tapes but not Beta tapes in the same store?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: We haven't done that, and...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: thing.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Because we are concerned with the overall effect we have broken down the ordinance to its logical components.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: establishments...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you have in the ordinance in fact exist in the real world out there,

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: They...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: video arcades only?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: ninety-one.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: bookstores only...

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correc-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: without arcades in them...

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: and so forth, every one of the categories, adult adult movies only?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: When you started out, though, there weren't all that many tapes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Actually, it's my unders-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: and you you you say that the combination of this list, you think of a bookstore, nowadays one can browse, one can even go to a cafe and have coffee, and all that, or go to a record shop and listen to the tape and then buy it.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The problem with your real-world description of a Barnes and Noble is, it doesn't exist in the adult entertainment world.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And you don't prevent them from...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you don't pre- prevent these stores from allowing customers to preview the films before they decide whether to buy them, the stores that sell adult films.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: If I understand the correct the question correctly, no.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Under the ordinance, can a customer come in and say, before I buy this film, I would like to see it, and the store manager says sure, go ahead, look at it first, then you can decide whether to buy it?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: For free?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: For free.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The ordinance permits that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: We are talking about...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But they want to charge for it.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: a- and the machines that allow you to view up to sixty clips, if you will, from various adult movies, or you go to another machine in another booth, you can I want to see that entire movie right there, as opposed to the clips.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: W- What is your principal argument in opposition to the rationale in the court of appeals? I I I have the feeling we're t- not letting you make the the principal argument here that you wanted to make.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The principal problem with the court of appeals decision is, of of course, how it requires what it requires, the use of of secondary effects and how it how it is discounts, if you will, or disregards this Court's precedent, is effectively what the court of appeals has said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The third...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: to impossible to do, why should be you be allowed, under the First Amendment, to do it? I mean, it it sounds as though you're you're you're kind of making an argument from spectral evidence here.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: The problem we were addressing was, is there a correlation, to use the term you use-, the word you used in Barnes...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: between the adult businesses and secondary effects, do they cause a problem.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: And and you've documented it, but as I understand it, you've documented it with reference to adult businesses which were not at the time defined or identified as necessarily being businesses that offered only one service, as opposed to a business like these, that offers two, so that the evidence, as I understand it, did not address the the issue that that is before us now.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: But it did address that issue, because it is not reasonable to assume that if you have two businesses side by side in the same block, causing secondary effects, that it can require them to disperse, which is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: entirely...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: we're th- we're, I think we're playing with words.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: But there's no evidence one way or the other...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that your problem?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: I don't believe it is i- it should be a problem.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You you measured you measured the effect of a video store what what do you call these, an arcade store, and a bookstore side-by-side, and you found that that was deleterious, and the problem is that you did not measure what the different effect would be if you knocked down the wall between them.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: That's the whole point that the city's been trying to make all along.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, you don't know...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: How do you know that, any more than I know it? I mean, I I ca- I see your I see your point when you say, look, if if there are two different entrepreneurs on a block, each of them doing whatever they can to draw in clientele, the odds are they're probably going to draw in a greater concentration of people than than than one.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: But you are describing this in terms of business organization.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Which is not how the city's zoning ordinance works.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And they, but the the the likelihood...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: that your nineteen seventy-eight evidence is going to bear on these various possibilities depends on more specific facts.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: If you look at the nature of the businesses, you come to an arcade to s- stay there and look, and when you leave that arcade, you have a different frame of mind than if you went to the bookstore, got your shrink-wrapped magazine, put it in a brown paper bag, and went home, so that's a, one f- logical deduction you can make from from all of the evidence that's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: before you.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: equally logical deduction that by looking at the cover of the shrink-wrapped magazine and and watching part of a video cli-, or a video clip of something that you ultimately buy, i- you're going to be left in about the same frame of mind in either case?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Aren't the maybe the magazine...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: covers are dull, but I assumed they were pretty racy, and if you look at sixty covers and sixty clip I don't know,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that's cutting it pretty fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice Souter, they're not live action. That's the difference.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: your question presented seems to be quite narrow.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the city did not study the combination but, rather, relied on judicially approved statutory precedent from other jurisdictions.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Correct, North Carolina.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Because the Ninth Circuit said that wasn't suitable reliance, is that right?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, basically what the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: thought that wouldn't do it because Hart addressed the health effects...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, what the Ninth what the Ninth, what the Ninth...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the effects on crime.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: What the Ninth Circuit effectively said was, there was some doubt whether Hart would survive scrutiny under this Court's Renton decision, but we are certainly confident that under Ninth Circuit precedent it's it's not a valid law.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I I don't know, it just struck me in reading the question that it was pretty much limited to whether they were right about whether you could rely on the Hart decision.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Hopefully not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: just as we...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Before the adoption of this amendment?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: That's correct, that in nineteen seventy-nine, our study supported a North Carolina statute prohibiting more than one adult business in the location, that that was a sufficient evidentiary basis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: thought...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: thought that Hart relied on the negative health effects of the arcade.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: We are less we, the city, is less concerned with the factual differences than with the legal reasoning and the fact that it fully supports, and should've been considered for that purpose by the Ninth Circuit, as validating the intent and purpose of the original ordinance and and eighty-three's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: amendment.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: getting down to is just how precise a particular study or a particular decision has to be.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: That's what th-, hopefully the ci-, well, that's what the city has been trying to do, but again, it goes back to, you know, i- if you look at at a- at your precedents, when when a city has these problems, the logical the logical thing for the city to do is say, ban them.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: And and where I'm a little confused is, at the at the beginning of this segment of the argument, when we began focusing on this question, I I thought you said something to the effect that we can't show this empirically, and I was surprised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: that's what you were going to tell, but you you your...

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: When you well, maybe I I I misspoke, but when you want to get into the empirical evidence of, are you a bad actor, this particular business is a bad actor, it's a very detailed nuisance type of of analysis, and when you have these combinations of adult businesses, or side-by-side adult businesses, and you try to do that same analysis, the immediate thing that is going to occur, the first thing that is going to occur, it's not me, it's my neighbor, it's not me, it's this other part of the business.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Let me ask you. Go ahead.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask you just one general question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: same location?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: analyzed  the solution.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: substantial number who f- who are similar to the one that we're talking about today?

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: I won't use an adverb to describe the amount.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: That's an adjective.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Excuse me, yes.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: May I go back to Justice O'Connor's question, because she she asked how the Hart facts could help you.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Well, again, t- the Hart reading of facts in in that i- i- in that decision, aga- go to the fact that that these combinations cause problems, and that the city's ordinance or excuse me, the city's study supported the inference that it's okay to the solution is to separate these...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: businesses.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the Hart, the as I under- am I am I, maybe I'm wrong, but I I thought the the basis for the governmental action in Hart was was public health based on the activity that was going on in these booths.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: In part, yes.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: If there are no further questions, I'd like to reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Klekner.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of other cities.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you know of any other cities that had the experience of businesses such as the two involved here, which were originally involved in one s- sex pandering activity, and then, after the ordinance was passed, went into a second one instead of opening up a sep- a separate store?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Justice Justice Scalia, you'll you'll...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I I mean, I mean, you know it...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: they may be unique, but the situation that they confronted may be unique as well.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: You'll pardon me for not adopting the reference of the Court in phrasing the nature of the business, but in fact the opposite is quite true.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's that's not how I read those pages.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Please.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Highland Book was issued a picture arcade police permit.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That this is this is the affidavit of the owner of both of these...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: businesses. That's correct. yes, that's...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: that's correct, Justice Scalia, but the point, the use of the term, adult business, was in the way that Los Angeles defined a use, and that if the Court will continue a little...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: bit further...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the only thing that's relevant here.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: No, it it it it with all respect, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: denominated adult.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: really another...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: business? It was ancillary to the other business? Right.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Up until a very recent time in Los Angeles history, one could carry up to fourty-nine percent of materials in one store and not be deemed to be an adult bookstore.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: This was this was a Los Angeles County ordinance...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: No that...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: you're referring to now?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: No, what I'm referring to is California court decisions which had interpreted the phrase, substantial number, amount of retail stock in trade.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: In the county ordinance? Ri- We're not talking about a State law, we're talking about a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: county ordinance?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: law, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: anything less...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: why did they co-, why were they dec- why did they construe that term?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: i- The term came fro- was an identical terminology utilized in many, many, ordinances throughout the State of California.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: This was a phrase used then in a number of different city and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: county ordinances in California?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Absolutely, and in fact it's interesting to note, because we've spoken about Hart this morning, that the North Carolina statute in Hart was one that pegged the definition of a retail bookstore as a preponderance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: bookstore, and th- that's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Wiener meant when he said in his affidavit, prior to June of nineteen ninety-one the only adult type of busi-, the only adult type of business use at Highland Books was its picture arcade which at all times exhibited exclusively...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: adult videotapes? And you think he...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: he had in mind that no more than fourty-nine percent of other business...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yeah, they...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: If I may read Yes. Thank you, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: The next sentence, You- , Justice Scalia, prior to June of nineteen ninety-one, the majority of the items available for retail sale at Highland Books were of a nonadult variety.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's why that's why the only adult business he was doing there was the other one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: variety. He's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He's not saying we were selling adult books, but selling less than fifty percent of adult books.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, forgive me, I can only tell you what the , what the circumstances were.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: use.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I appreciate your testimony to that effect, but what his affidavit clearly says is that the only adult business he did prior to nineteen ninety-one was the e- exhibition of adult videotapes.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Forgive me, Justice Scalia, I don't want to belabor the point but that's not a fair reading of it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: offered.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't understand what this fifty-one fourty-nine pertains to.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Stock in trade of the retail establishment.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, and and it means that if fourty-nine if if if less than fifty-one percent is adult material,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: you're not...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: at the time, in Los Angeles, it was not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: characterized as an adult use Adult use or business, that's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but it doesn't mean that if you have fifty-one percent in one adult business and fourty-nine percent in another adult business, the fourty-nine percent is not an adult business?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: The fourty-nine percent of adult retail material did acted to let me , if I may phrase it the other way.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Fifty-one percent of stock in trade at a business in Los Angeles, if it was adult, made that store adult, even if it was twenty thousand total items, and virtually t-, and nearly ten thousand of them had nothing to do with sexually oriented...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: material.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: West- I understand that that division, that that was by inventory not by sales.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: problems was, you had lots of books, dull books that nobody was going to buy, and that you had that was what you had for fifty-one percent, and then you had the stuff that people bought, and that was the fourty-nine percent.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: The record does not reflect that, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: been true.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: that it would not have been considered an adult bookstore so long as they maintained that fifty-one...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct,

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: a- i- if I may, as in Hart, Mr. Wiener's initial businesses, as they were initially constituted, did not conflict with the multiple use provision, notwithstanding the fact that a hundred percent of the videotapes viewed in them were adult, and fourty-nine percent or up to fourty-nine percent of the retail items available for sale were also adult.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But can I...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: That did not constitute two adult uses under California law at that time, and that is exactly what the law was in Hart, that it was perfectly permissible to have a hundred percent adult arcade material, and fourty-nine percent retail books, videos and so forth, and that would not have conflicted with the prohibition in Hart.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Could you can you explain I'm just trying to get back to the main point here, I at least as I understand it,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: don't assume I know a lot of the detail that you know.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: If it were a meat store which said, over-the-counter is one thing, at the restaurant is another of course we could do it a million ways.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: But we have to do it some way, and so forget whether there's evidence or not.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: But they say, either way, we're just trying to have a reasonable definition.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Well, initially.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of the combined uses.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: that's a bad analogy, because auto places are not try liquor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: on its face. So...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: let's assume it in their favor that way, and then argue...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Let's take a look at that one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I assume...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: But th- but this whole question is something that's secondary.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: the only question is whether or not this dual business draws more people, and whether or not there's an empirical basis for it.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: I'm not quite sure about drawing more people.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Los Angeles was concerned...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: the arcade because it didn't draw any more people?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: No, it wasn't people, it was the notion for a variety of reasons, and if we return to Young versus American Mini Theatres, there is at least there's some indication in Young that it's not simply a question of people, but rather, what is the perception from the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: outside world?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: not simply a question of people, but surely the kind of of of secondary effects that have been spoken of of in cases depend upon people's activities, and the people aren't ten miles away, they're somehow associated with the business.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of a dozen, or fifteen or twenty unrelated adult businesses.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: don't you think the legislature is entitled to draw what might be common sense inferences from the studies that it made, and that it doesn't have to have empirical evidence for every single thing that it does in the ordinance?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Of course not, Your Honor, and for example, if the city, as it suggests, which we do not agree with, really thought I I I I don't mean to cast aspersions on the city.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: has to be.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: i- i- incidentally on the other point, I I I reviewed your footnote.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank you, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'm going to go home now.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It seems to me that that if if you're objecting to the to the municipality's use of of this other study, which didn't treat combined stores, you r- it seems to me you you you have to tell us why it it would be likely that combining the diverse businesses under one roof could make any difference.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: There are a number of responses to that, and and and they require a bit of e- articulation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But but it doesn't have...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to say that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But that's no different. But that's no...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: different, in as, Justice Scalia, from a single seven eleven store that says, bananas for sale, ninety-nine cents today, rutabagas a dollar and a half, and Coca-Colas on sale...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: here. It's on-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: respect, would you be making the same argument that you are now making for tapes plus arcade, sales of tapes plus arcade, if the combined uses were a massage parlor and an adult hotel?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: That's a that's a very fair and good question, and I've been thinking about that one for a long, long time, and I think the easy answer to it is simply that with respect to the massage parlor, there would be no First Amendment interest to protect, and therefore it wouldn't raise the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, I guess you'd say the...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: same thing about the adult motel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: up- u- Absolutely, and a- absolutely...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: A-, and in fact I did say that in FW PBS versus City of Dallas, when one of the three components was an adult motel, but interestingly and and and I'll try to address the combination question, but interestingly, and this is one of the things that makes the LA su- study suspect to begin with, is that of the eighty-eigt adult uses that were focused on by the police department and the planning department in Hollywood, eigh-, fourty-one of them I I beg your pardon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or adult motels.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: that question is fairly subsumed by the, in the question presented.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: No, you're you're exactly right, Mr. Chief Justice, there wasn't a challenge specifically to it, but as we...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: There wasn't a challenge specifically to it.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: The specific challenge that was made was whether the study supported the city's stated purpose that in combining a bookstore and an arcade in one establishment, in one physical structure, gene- caused the kinds of problems, the only problems which the study evaluated and found and, in fact, in the city's own s- papers it makes clear, and we have set this forth in page two of the respondent's appendix, at at, we we note to it, rather, in joint appendix one oh nine, that on the contrary, this st- the...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Respondent's appendix...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The red brief, in page two?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Forgive me, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But you gave it...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: you gave an answer th- that I didn't think was just a glib lawyer's answer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Massages don't.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Right,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and I...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: combining a motion picture theater and a bookstore?

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: I would I t- t- I would one would think that the same kind of analogy as we have offered here would be present, that with a motion picture theater and a bookstore there would appear to be no logical suggestion that the two of them would generate any greater secondary effects than the motion picture theater alone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: area...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: differently.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Well, I'm not sure that the separate ownership is what constitutes the the vice aimed at at the ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and the...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: if you have a seven eleven and an A and P next door, it's the same problem as if they're owned by the same owner.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I mean, it seems to me that that those two businesses operated by separate owners would have precisely the same secondary effect as a single business operating both a motion picture and a and a bookstore.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: I'm not aware of any data that would suggest that the combination would the combination in in, Justice Stevens, in in your hypothetical that a theater and a bookstore together, I'm not aware of any data that would lead one to reasonably conclude that the combination of a retail use in a motion picture theater would generate more secondary effects than what I assume would be the more intense use, the motion picture theater operating alone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, you conceded a moment ago Go ahead.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: may be true if you interpreted secondary effects as just this look that the neighborhood has by having s- the businesses strung along the block, but if secondary effects is also the number of people come who loiter, who who who drive around, who who c- who c- concentrate outside.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: I think that's fair, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, but isn't one way to...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: pose that question to pose it the way Justice Kennedy did? The the the the the question is, does the particular combination increase the degree of clientele, because we know that the greater the clientele, the greater the crime rate is going to be, and so there there seems to me to be a factual basis for saying that the clientele is a kind of a surrogate measure for those very effects that the city can legitimately try to counteract.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: With all respect, Justice Souter, I must question the hypothesis that it is the clientele that will cause the problems.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I would sug-

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: The amount of clientele is associated with the problems.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I I I really...

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: don't think so in this instance, because if that were, in fact, the hypothesis of the city, surely, with all of the thought that they have given to this, there would've been an absolute size limitation or a square footage limitation that was part of the legislation to deal just with that problem, but they haven't.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah, well maybe maybe they could do that.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: A- And I suppose we'll be back...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: here and then having to address that.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: You seem you seem to be arguing that we should decide this case on the assumption that the city's evidence, as it now exists, would not be a sustainable basis for an ordinance saying, you can't have two you can't have twenty-five different adult uses in one arcade.

turns.name: JOHN H. WESTON
turns.text: Not not at all, Justice Souter.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Weston.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But, if you co- you combine...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: buying it, they get out of the store.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Well, that's th- they're you cannot expect an ordinance to solve everything...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in in...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: questioning whether there's going to be more of a problem if you have the stand-alone arcade, where to see the film to the bitter end you have to stay there, but you really like it, so you buy it, and you get out quicker.

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: Well, but that's an argument to getting rid of r- rid the arcades.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ordinances is to spread out...

turns.name: MICHAEL L. KLEKNER
turns.text: spread out the patronage, to to to make sure that you don't have this clustering, the, this magnet effect.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Klekner.
